messages.content: Hypothetical - In the vien of an Internet creepypasta.   Write an account of the 'passanger' that got off a tube train, only to find a town that was a decade behind nominal 'real' time.

I still remember the look of utter confusion plastered on the man's face when he stepped off the train. His bewildered expression was the last thing I expected to find in the monotony of my daily commute on the London Underground. I didn't pay much attention at first—we all run into odd characters on the Tube now and then—but as I sat there, viewing his disoriented demeanor, curiosity got the better of me.

His clothes were quite ordinary, a dated fashion perhaps, but nothing too outlandish. It was his reaction to everything around him that made me really sit up and take notice. He examined his phone with a puzzled frown, poking at the screen as if it were some alien technology. Deciding I had a few minutes to spare before the next train, I followed him out of the station, onto the bustling street.

It was then that he whispered to himself, but loud enough for my ears, "What year is this?"

The town outside the station seemed exactly as it always did, people rushing through their erratically synchronized lives, coffee in one hand, the morning's burdens in the other. But to this stranger, it all appeared alien.

Curiosity turned into an itch I couldn't ignore, and I approached him. "Are you okay, mate?" I asked.

His response sent a chill down my spine. "I... I got on the train in 2013. I just got off—I thought it was the next stop." His voice was shaky. "Everything is different."

Thinking it was some sort of prank or else he needed medical attention, I glanced at my phone for the date: 2023. The idea of time travel was too absurd to entertain, but there was genuine fear and confusion in his eyes.

I decided to play along, if only to ease his mind. "It's 2023. You're in London." 

The color drained from his face. "But... that's impossible. I must have fainted, maybe this is some dream."

Still uncertain about his state of mind, I encouraged him to have a coffee with me. Perhaps I could dig deeper into the mystery. He accepted, his movements indicating he was mesmerized by the world around him yet fundamentally out of place.

As we walked, every advertisement, every model of car, every scrap of fashion screamed 'advancement' to him, confirming the decade he had seemingly skipped. He spoke of his life in 2013, with striking detail, nothing like dreams or the vague ramblings of a madman.

Once seated in a nearby café that played songs unfamiliar to his ears, his story unraveled, becoming surreal with every word. He mentioned getting on the Tube after a mundane day at work, expecting to arrive home within the hour. He recalled the lull of the train, the distinct hum of the rails, and then, standing up as his stop approached, darkness encircling his vision before he stumbled out into this 'future'.

He carved out the eerie sensation of lost time with desperate clarity, looking at me, not so much seeking answers but simply yearning to be heard, understood—believing himself, perhaps more than trying to convince me.

With the coffee growing cold between us and his tale at an end, I offered to help find his family, maybe seek medical advice. But it was evident he felt utterly disconnected—not just from time but from the reality he knew. His gaze lingered on the conflicting sights, an ancient remnant observing an unknown age.

Before more could be said, he stood, thanked me, and insisted he needed to walk, process the unfathomable shift he faced. I watched as he left, his silhouette eventually swallowed by the crowds—a ghost, an enigma, a passenger to a time beyond his own.

The account would later grip the online world, a modern urban myth that propagated through forums and message boards. Theories abounded, from rifts in the space-time continuum to elaborate hoaxes. But no one ever saw the man again, his existence as transient as a shadow at dusk, leaving an indelible echo in the digital corridors of the internet—a creepypasta legend that disturbs and captivates to this day.
Creepypasta :   Do not ask what Wikipetan's birthday is :)

**Title: The Unspoken Anniversary**

In the deepest, darkest corners of the web, where knowledge intertwines with madness, there's an old tale shared amongst avid users of the wiki. The story concerns Wikipetan, an unofficial moe anthropomorphic personification of Wikipedia, often depicted as a lively young girl. But beneath her cheerful, informative exterior, a sinister secret was said to lurk.

It was a typical night on the internet, filled with ceaseless browsing and the pursuit of endless knowledge, when a new thread emerged on an obscure message board known for its unsettling stories and digital folklore. The thread was titled, "Do not ask what Wikipetan's birthday is :)" and it contained only a cryptic message:

"Curiosity can be your demise. For those who seek forbidden knowledge, beware the truth behind the innocent. Wikipetan's birth date holds a key—a key that should never turn the lock to which it fits. Heed this warning, or descend into an abyss from which there is no escape."

Most users dismissed the thread as yet another attempt at a viral creepypasta, a cheap thrill to be consumed and forgotten. Comments ranged from skeptical scoffs to intrigued musings, but there was one user, a newcomer to the board, who couldn't resist the pull of the unknown.

This user, who went by the pseudonym "Alexios321," responded with a taunt, defying the warning. "I'll bite, this has to be a joke. I'll just look it up."

Several members replied to Alexios321 instantly, their text bearing an urgency unlike the forum's usual laid-back banter.

"Stop, don't go any further!"
"It's not too late to turn back!"
"You don't understand what you're messing with!"

But it was too late. Alexios321 had already begun their search, delving into articles and archives for any trace of Wikipetan's birthday. As minutes turned to hours, the forum fell silent, the playful banter replaced by a cold stillness.

Suddenly, a private message popped up on Alexios321's screen. It was from a user who had never posted before, their username simply a jumble of numbers and letters. The message contained a date and a single line: "Happy Birthday, Wikipetan."

The moment Alexios321 read that date aloud, their screen flickered violently. System errors cascaded across the display, and a high-pitched noise screeched from the speakers. The computer crashed, refusing to reboot.

The next day, there was no sign of Alexios321 on the message board, or anywhere else on the internet, for that matter. After the incident, the thread mysteriously vanished, as if it had never existed. Attempts to retrieve it led only to dead ends and blank pages.

Whispers among board members suggested that those who learned of Wikipetan's birthday found themselves confronted by a terrible knowledge that unhinged their grasp on reality. Others speculated that they had stumbled into an obscure digital trap, their online presence wiped from existence.

Whether these occurrences are the result of a powerful cyber entity, a collective hallucination, or something far more inexplicable, one thing is universally acknowledged: no one asks about Wikipetan's birthday anymore, and those who know are wise to keep silent. For there are truths out there in cyberspace that are best left undiscovered, and some birthdays that are never to be celebrated—or spoken of again.
Another creepypasta - If you plagarise from Wikipedia , you get an e-mail image or instagram from Wikipetan crying ...

**Title: The Tears of Wikipetan**

In a digital age where information flows as freely as water, the temptation to take what is not yours is a pitfall many an unwary student has stumbled into. The community guidelines of Wikipedia are clear and well-intentioned, promoting the free and honest distribution of knowledge. But there exists a tale on the internet, spoken in hushed tones among those who have dedicated themselves to the pursuit of information—a tale that serves as a chilling warning against the sin of plagiarism.

Jake was a regular college student who, like many others, was overwhelmed by deadlines and the pressure to excel. In desperation to meet an assignment deadline, he made a choice that all too many have made before him—he plagiarized content from Wikipedia. It was easy, too easy. Copy, paste, and the essay was done. Sure, he felt a twinge of guilt, but it was quickly overshadowed by the relief of submission. He had gotten away with it—or so he thought.

The legend goes that within hours of his transgression, Jake received an email from an unrecognizable address. Its subject was a string of numbers and letters, indecipherable and seemingly random. The body of the email was empty, save for an attached image. Morbid curiosity led him to open the attachment. There, on the screen, was Wikipetan, the unofficial personification of Wikipedia. The girl, usually depicted with a smile, offering knowledge freely, was now crying bitter tears that seemed to blur and distort the image with their intensity.

Jake's unease grew with each passing second as he stared at the screen. It was just an illustration, but it felt as though her sorrow was directed at him, an accusation of his moral failing. To cast the unsettling feeling aside, he deleted the email, attributing it to some elaborate prank.

Brushing off the incident, Jake continued about his day until he received a notification—a direct message on Instagram. He slid open the message to find yet another image of Wikipetan, the same sad expression in her eyes, but this time she was holding up a sign, the words written upon it sending a jolt of dread through Jake's body: "I know what you did."

Panicked, Jake replied to the message, but it was as if he was conversing with a phantom—his messages met with silence. That night, he received another email, this time with only a link. The link led to an edit history, an exact record of the content he plagiarized. Below it, in bold, a new addition had been written:

"To copy one's labor is to extinguish knowledge's flame. Correct your mistake before the tears drown more than just your conscience."

The warning was clear. Jake couldn't sleep, hounded by pangs of guilt and an inexplicable fear. In the early hours of morning, bleary-eyed and shaken, he rewrote his essay—this time in his own words, with proper citations and references. He resubmitted it to his professor, confessing to his initial misconduct.

The next day, rumor has it that Jake received one last message from Wikipetan. This time, the image was different. Now, she was smiling, her storyline tears dried, and with them, the ghostly presence that had haunted him seemed to dissipate.

Jake never spoke much of the incident, but he was a changed student. He worked diligently and respected the sanctity of information sharing from that point forward. But the specter of what he had witnessed lingered in his memory, a stark reminder of the consequences of taking what isn't yours, even from as vast and forgiving a repository as Wikipedia.

The story of Jake's encounter with the crying Wikipetan spread like a digital myth, a modern cautionary tale about academic integrity and the mysterious guardians of the internet's collective wisdom. In forums and chat rooms, they say that to this day, Wikipetan watches over the sprawling labyrinths of Wikipedia, ever-vigilant for those who would sully its purpose. And for those who succumb to the temptation of plagiarism, it is said they will find themselves haunted by her mournful gaze—a digital specter weeping for the theft of knowledge's greatest gift: truth.
As with any internet urban legend there are various variants in regard to  other user misconduct related to Wikipedia that have got tearful Wikipetan  e-mails, instagrams or vanishing tik-toks..

**Title: The Weeping Keeper of Wisdom**

Urban legends are like living organisms, mutating and adapting to the digital ecosystem. The tale of the weeping Wikipetan did not stop with Jake and his act of plagiarism. As the story spread, mutations of the legend emerged, each variant connected by a single thread—misuse of Wikipedia and the subsequent appearance of a tearful Wikipetan.

### Variant 1: The Vandal's Regret

In this version of the legend, a notorious wiki vandal, nicknamed 'TaggerTom,' reveled in defacing articles with egregious lies and crude humor. His latest spree of digital graffiti seemed to go unnoticed, until he received an email with the subject, "Why would you hurt me?" The image attached was Wikipetan, her face streaked with tears, holding the defaced articles before her broken heart. From that day forward, every account 'TaggerTom' used to vandalize was promptly banned. The once brazen troublemaker quietly retired his ways, replaced by a newfound respect for the integrity of shared knowledge. Some say he even became an editor, righting the wrongs of others who might follow in his misguided footsteps.

### Variant 2: The Translator's Despair

Further abroad, a different account unfolded involving 'PolyglotPete,' a bi-lingual user who deliberately mistranslated content to mislead non-English speakers. Pete found amusement in his subtle sabotage, until the day he received an Instagram direct message that stopped him cold—a video clip of Wikipetan, tears streaming down her cheeks, as she pointed to the inaccuracies, her expression pleading for truth. The message was simply, "Lost in translation?" but its impact was profound. 'PolyglotPete' corrected all his translations and pledged to use his skills to bridge language barriers honestly, fearing another sorrowful plea from the custodian of knowledge.

### Variant 3: The Whisper of Deletion

Sometimes, the legend took on a quieter tone. 'WikiWill,' a user with a self-appointed mission to nominate articles for deletion, derived joy from stripping the wiki of what he deemed 'unworthy' content. In his wake, articles vanished, along with the hard work of countless contributors. It was after extinguishing a particularly beloved page that he found a TikTok notification—a short loop of Wikipetan, holding a candle that flickered and dwindled as she wept, the hashtag #EveryArticleMatters clinging to the bottom of the screen. Uncertain whether it was a warning or a curse, 'WikiWill' became the staunchest defender of niche articles, fighting to keep even the most obscure knowledge alive within Wikipedia's digital walls.

As these stories travel through chat rooms, Reddit threads, and forum posts, the message of the weeping Wikipetan remains the same: respect the collective effort behind the curation of human knowledge. Whether it's a tale of vandals, deceivers, or overzealous gatekeepers, anyone who dares to compromise the sanctity of the Wiki may find themselves subject to a visitation by the crying avatar, a wordless guardian embodying the silent sobs of a wisdom wronged.

With each retelling, the legend of Wikipetan grows, a modern parable of digital responsibility. But whether she truly patrols the meta-data planes or exists merely as an allegory woven by the community, the effect is tangible—promoting a respect for the unparalleled resource we have in Wikipedia, and a reminder of the fragile human effort that binds it together.
A variant - A user posted copyrighted anime material to Wikimedia Commons..   This got not only Wikipetan, but her younger sister Commons-tan as well ! (Spooky?)

**Title: The Copyrighted Shadows**

Deep in the virtual catacombs of the internet, where the lines between permission and piracy blur, a new variant of the Wikipetan legend manifested, sending a shiver down the spines of content creators and curators alike.

The subject of this grim tale was a well-known uploader in the Wikimedia Commons community, a user by the name of 'FotoPhantom.' He had a reputation for sourcing high-quality images, and his contributions had always enhanced the visual aspect of many Wikipedia articles. However, FotoPhantom's insatiable desire for recognition led him down a darker path. The lure of uploading a series of exclusive, high-resolution anime stills proved too tempting, and he carelessly disregarded the specter of copyright.

As these vivid images made their rounds across the Wiki landscape, garnering praise and admiration, an ominous cloud began to form on the digital horizon. That evening, under the shadowy veil of dusk, FotoPhantom received an enigmatic email containing an animated .gif file. Upon opening it, he was met with the haunting sight of two figures—Wikipetan and a less familiar, youthful companion, Commons-tan, the personification of Wikimedia Commons.

The .gif played over and over, showcasing the sisters in a scene that chilled FotoPhantom to the core. Wikipetan, her face stricken with sorrow, was shedding her customary tears that seemed to seep through the screen. Beside her, Commons-tan wept inconsolably, clutching the copyrighted anime stills to her chest. The darkness around them appeared alive, shifting and writhing as though the shadows themselves were disapproving spectators.

But the most unsettling part of the image was not their tears—it was their eyes, or rather, the lack thereof. Where their vibrant gazes should have been were just vacant pools, as though their very essence had been siphoned away by the infringement of intellectual property rights. A whispered message could almost be heard emanating from the image: "What is shared freely must not be chained."

Paralyzed by fear and overwhelmed with guilt, FotoPhantom realized the gravity of his mistake. By morning, he had removed all copyrighted content from his uploads, replacing them with apologies and vows to advocate for creative rights. Yet the .gif remained in his inbox, a haunting reminder. When opened now, it showed nothing but two pairs of eyes, sorrowful and seemingly forgiving, gazing from the depths of the animation.

Word of FotoPhantom's ordeal spread among contributors as another chilling slice of digital folklore. Whether taken as truth or merely an allegory for the respect of intellectual property, the tale captured the collective consciousness of the community.

To this day, the specter of the weeping sisters lingers. Some say that when a user unlawfully uploads copyrighted material, the shadows grow restless, and the ghostly images may visit again, serving as a spectral safeguard of artistic expression and integrity.

This eerie addition to the Wikipetan legend serves not just as a macabre tale to swap in hushed tones across message boards but as a spectral warning. For in the vast and venerable expanse of Wikimedia, the sanctity of shared knowledge and creativity hinges upon the respect for the rights of each individual creator. And to those who fail to heed this credence, Wikipetan and Commons-tan stand as silent sentinels, their absence of eyes reflecting the blindness infringed upon the very spirit of open-source collaboration.
There are rumors other wikis have their equivalents..   some have not been confirmed in the wild so to speak...

**Title: The Guardians Beyond Wikipedia**

As the internet sprawls out like a boundless digital tapestry, there are rumors that for each specialized wiki that exists in its far-flung corners, there is an equivalent guardian or entity, much like Wikipetan. However, sightings of these other guardians are elusive and often wrapped in a heavy shroud of mystery, their tales passed on in quiet posts and whispered among regular contributors with a mixture of respect and superstition.

### The Archive Apparition of Wikia

In the diverse world of Fandom's Wikia, contributors speak of an Archive Apparition that roams the myriad of wikis found there. This spectral curator is said to appear to those who recklessly delete or mar historical pages and posts. The Archive Apparition takes the form of iridescent code strings, wrapping around cherished archives to protect them. Those who've reported encounters often say they feel an overwhelming sense of history's weight and a calling to preserve rather than obliterate.

### The Repository Revenant of WikiSource

WikiSource, a digital library of free texts, has its whispers of a Repository Revenant that safeguards the integrity of the documents within. Contributors who try to alter the texts with modern bias or inaccurate information report an uncanny chill enveloping their workspace, as if a ghostly librarian had passed through. A soft rustling, like the turning of ancient pages, fills the air until the corrupted entries are rectified, as if the revenant itself is rewriting the wrongs inflicted upon the literary annals.

### The Semantic Specter of Semantic MediaWiki

Semantic MediaWiki, a platform allowing structured data to be managed and queried, is said to be watched over by the Semantic Specter. Those who misuse semantic tags or purposely input faulty data speak of their interfaces becoming unnaturally slow and their queries returning cryptic messages. This specter's form is imagined as a flickering presence of disrupted data streams, a guardian of the logical and structural order so vital to the efficacy of Semantic MediaWiki.

### The Fact Phantom of Wiktionary

Wiktionary's guardian is rumored to be the Fact Phantom, a silent watcher ensuring the dictionary's linguistic verity. Those who've attempted to insert slang or fabricated words with no etymological foundation have experienced their text reverting in real-time, corrections appearing as if by an invisible hand. Whispered accounts from these users suggest that the Fact Phantom fills their consciousness with an inexplicable knowledge of words' true roots and a compulsion to maintain linguistic purity.

### The Keeper of Britannica's Knowledge

Even outside of the Wikimedia sphere, on platforms like Encyclopedia Britannica, legend speaks of the Keeper of Knowledge—a more regal and ancient entity that embodies the long tradition of scholarly accuracy. Those who bring hearsay and conjecture instead of vetted facts to Britannica's hallowed pages might encounter a Grecian-robed figure in digital form, erasing their falsehoods and replacing them with venerable truths.

In these reported manifestations, whether they are true specters or the shared hallucinations of overworked and imaginative contributors, there lies a common theme. Each entity upholds the ideals of its respective domain—accuracy, preservation, integrity, and respect for humanity's collected wisdom.

Yet, for all the stories that circulate within the insular communities of dedicated wiki volunteers, sightings of these guardians are unconfirmed, their existence regarded as part of the folklore that naturally accrues in places deeply revered for their knowledge. These legends serve as parables for the ethics of information-sharing, with their mythical keepers emerging from the collective consciousness of the internet to remind us of the responsibilities we bear in sustaining the integrity of the digital world.
Crying Wikipetan variant :  - A 'fringe'  pusher got a Wikipetan (in a lab coat) with a "And the earth is flat?" sarcasm look...

**Title: The Sardonic Scientist of Sourced Wisdom**

On the digital battleground of factual versus fringe, a new variant of the crying Wikipetan myth emerged, coalescing into a cautionary tale for those who tilt at the windmills of established science with unproven or discredited theories.

The subject of this eerie mutation was 'FringeFreddy', a user who fervently pushed pseudoscientific claims into the heart of Wikipedia's scientific articles. With zeal, he cited dubious sources and inserted paragraphs of text supporting the Flat Earth theory into pages about the planet's geology and space travel. For a while, FringeFreddy managed to evade the watchful eyes of diligent editors, his guerrilla tactics causing momentary disruptions in the fabric of verified knowledge.

One evening, as FringeFreddy prepared to launch another salvo of fringe edits, his inbox pinged with the arrival of an unexpected email. The subject line was a prickly taunt in the form of a question, "And the earth is flat?" Curiosity piqued and pride wounded by the provocation, FringeFreddy clicked open the email.

There, staring back at him from his screen, was Wikipetan, but unlike any representation he had ever seen. She was clad in a pristine white lab coat, her hair pinned back with scientific precision, and the corners of her mouth turned up in a smirk that radiated sarcasm. In her hand was a globe, and her eyes—those usually warm and inviting eyes—bore into him with a cool "Really?" that cut deeper than words could.

Beside the image, a simple caption written in a font reminiscent of chalk on a blackboard read, "Common sense is not so common."

FringeFreddy was taken aback—his beloved conspiracy so casually dismissed by the mascot for the very repository he sought to enlighten with 'hidden truths'. The mockery stung, but it was the look in Wikipetan's eyes that truly haunted him. It was as if she were challenging him to question the validity of his convictions, to test them against the rigorous standards of the scientific method, and to look beyond the echo chamber of fringe beliefs.

The encounter was a turning point. FringeFreddy's subsequent edits shifted dramatically. He began cleaning up the articles he once defaced, this time adding properly sourced and credible information. After all, he still had his passion for the subjects, but now he approached them with a renewed emphasis on evidence and peer review.

The story of FringeFreddy's confrontation with a sardonically skeptical Wikipetan spread quietly among Wikipedia's editors—a tale tinged with scientific rigor and a dash of otherworldly chastisement. Whether spawned from the depths of his own conscience, a clever ruse by another editor, or something more mystical, the image of the lab coat-clad Wikipetan served as a spectral guardian of scientific integrity.

Amid the legends of the weeping Wikipetan came this unique specter, one that didn't evoke tears but wielded the power of skepticism—a vision warning against the danger of allowing unverified beliefs to masquerade as equal to science, and a reminder that even in an age of information, the flat Earths of the mind can still be roundly challenged.
Crying Wikipetan variant -   Unsourced claims..   The Wikipetan message was " I don't know who to believe!"...

**Title: The Paradox of the Unsubstantiated**

In the vast expanse of cyberspace, where the line between fact and fabrication often grows thin, a new variant story circulates among the Wikipedia editing community. This tale involves an editor known simply as "UnsourcedUlysses," who had a penchant for adding unsourced claims to articles—sprinkling them liberally like seeds without soil, with little thought to their veracity.

Unlike the more blatantly malicious vandals or conspiracy theorists, UnsourcedUlysses operated under a misguided belief that his additions were "probably true" or "made sense," neglecting the foundational Wikipedia principle that every claim must be verifiable and cited from reliable sources.

His trail of unsubstantiated information went unnoticed at first, lost in the sheer volume of daily edits. That was until UnsourcedUlysses decided to take on a more prominent topic, injecting a series of unsourced "facts" into the body of an article about a well-known historical event. This action prompted Wikipedia's diligent guardians of the truth to take swift corrective action.

But it wasn't the standard warning or reprimand message that caught UnsourcedUlysses off guard—it was something far more disconcerting. That night, he received an email with a simple yet cryptic message: "I don't know who to believe!" Opening the email, he found an image of Wikipetan unlike any clip art or cheerful illustration that normally adorned her persona. She was sitting atop a pile of books and papers, her face buried in her hands as if overwhelmed by distress. From between the gaps of her fingers, tears cascaded onto the scattered documents below.

The caption below read, "Truth loses its way when unfounded claims lead the path. Seek sources, seek certainty." Her sorrow was tangible; it was the grief of misinformation gone rampant, a wisdom brought to tears by the very confusion she was conceived to dispel.

The image haunted UnsourcedUlysses. It became all he could think about when he approached the keyboard to edit. Could it be that his actions were seeding doubt rather than knowledge? That his unsourced sentences were cracking the pillars of the temple of verifiable truth?

With a spirit of contrition, UnsourcedUlysses began to revisit his contributions, diligently adding citations and removing information that couldn't be confirmed. The legendary email had served as both a spectral rebuke and a clarion call back to the principles of responsible editing.

Word of this variant encounter with a weeping Wikipetan spread quietly yet resoundingly within the inner circles of Wikipedia editors, solidifying itself as a gentle yet ghostly reminder. For in the realm of shared knowledge, where trust is a currency more valuable than gold, the demand for sources is the guardian against the chaos of misinformation.

Wikipetan's tearful image became an emblem, a lesson to those who tread the corridors of information, a whisper in the digital winds—source your facts, provide evidence, and uphold the sanctity of trusted knowledge, lest you too find yourself at the mercy of disbelief’s paralyzing grip.
Crying Wikipetan variant  Block evasion… The Wikipetan message was " I said No! the first time!" ..

**Title: Echoes of Refusal**

Behind the curtain of anonymity offered by the internet, some see the rules as mere suggestions, lines drawn in the digital sand to be crossed and re-crossed at will. Such was the mindset of "BanDodgerBenny," a once-legitimate Wikipedia editor who found himself on the wrong side of community guidelines, leading to a well-deserved block.

But Benny was undeterred. He viewed the block not as a lesson but as a challenge. Changing IP addresses and creating sock puppet accounts, Benny slipped back into the editing pool, undulating through the wiki waters like a digital eel evading capture. His edits weren't malicious; they were, as he saw them, beneficial. But Benny had ignored the fundamental rule: bans were there for a reason, a consequence of actions that undermined the trust and order crucial to the Wikipedia community.

His evasion lasted longer than most, a testament to his guile and understanding of the system. That was until he pushed too far, attempting to delete a heated discussion from his original account's talk page via a new puppet. It was as if the act of trying to erase his own history was a step too far, a transgression that broke through to the other side of the digital veil.

The very night of his audacious edit, Benny received a message that froze his blood colder than any administrator's warning. There, in his inbox, was an email simply titled, "I said No! the first time!" Attached was an image of Wikipetan, her typical bright smile replaced with a furrowed brow and a finger pointed sternly in disapproval. Her eyes conveyed a mother's exhaustion, a teacher's exasperation, and a judge's finality rolled into one.

The message was clear: you've been barred, and trying to circumvent the decision was unacceptable. But beyond that, there was a deeper lesson in Wikipetan's expression. It was a call for integrity, for accepting consequences, and for respecting boundaries. Her solitary tear wasn't of sadness—it was born of frustration, the kind that comes when one must repeatedly set the same rule for a child who refuses to listen.

BanDodgerBenny sat there, fixated on the image of a disappointed, weeping Wikipetan. The message hit home harder than any autoblock could. He realized his evasion was not only a violation of policies but, worse, a breach of trust, something far harder to rebuild than any encyclopedic entry.

The tale of BanDodgerBenny and the unyielding Wikipetan spread like an electronic legend, whispered in editing forums and even chuckled about in admin circles—a ghost story with a moral backbone. Beyond a simple account of block evasion and digital reprimand, this variant of the crying Wikipetan myth stood as an allegory for the value of accountability—a reminder that sometimes "No" means no, and second chances must be earned with honesty, not subterfuge.

Her image endures, shared between editors both as an in-joke for those who respect the system and a spectral deterrent for those considering defiance—a legend of a digital guardian whose tears are as much for what Wikipedia stands for as they are for those who would undermine it.
In contrast some users have  gotten enigmatic smiling Wikipetan for excellent edits and efforts..  rarer but some examples might be listable..

**Title: The Smiling Sentinel of the Wiki**

Amid the whispered tales of spectral warnings and prohibitive admonishments from a sobbing Wikipetan, there exists a far less publicized but deeply cherished narrative. This series of stories, spread by word-of-mouth and through the quiet back channels of user talk pages, celebrate the rare but cherished sightings of a smiling Wikipetan—a harbinger of approval and encouragement for those whose dedication to Wikipedia goes above and beyond.

### The Curator's Kudos

One editor, known for meticulously curating articles on endangered species, dedicated months to researching, writing, and referencing, elevating the quality of content to remarkable heights. After finally completing a featured article candidate that was unanimously praised and promoted, they received an unexpected email. The only content was a simple animated image of Wikipetan giving a thumbs up, a broad grin upon her face, and holding a sign that read, "Extinction is forever, but your edits are eternal." The editor, usually quite private and reserved, shared the image with a few close wiki-friends, and the legend of Wikipetan's approving presence took root.

### The Barnstar Manifestation

In another account, a long-time contributor who specialized in translating articles from English to several other languages was awarded a series of barnstars—Wikipedia's tokens of appreciation for exemplary work. That night, the translator found their user page graced by a delightful digital drawing. It depicted a beaming Wikipetan clapping her hands as barnstars floated around her. The caption charmingly read, "Lost in translation? Found in appreciation!" This visual pat on the back became a story shared in hushed tones, a modern-mythic tip of the hat from the muse of free knowledge herself.

### The Anti-Vandal Vanguard

Perhaps the most famous of these benevolent visitations centered on a group of anti-vandalism patrol volunteers who had coordinated a 24-hour watch during a high-risk event, successfully keeping the site's integrity intact. In the cooldown after the whirlwind of reverts, each member found a personalized message in their inbox: an image of Wikipetan, a smile as wide as the horizon, proudly holding a shield with the Wikipedia logo, alongside the words, "Guardians of truth—your vigilance preserves us all." The united volunteers cherished this rare sign of acknowledgement, an enduring testament to their collective efforts.

### The Archivist's Affirmation

A lesser-known tale speaks of an archivist who spent years ensuring the proper sourcing and categorization of historical photographs uploaded to Wikimedia Commons. Over time, they built a repository of invaluable imagery, shedding light on forgotten chapters of history. One morning, they were greeted with a private message containing an image of Wikipetan, wearing a historian's cap, her joyous smile accompanied by a sparkle in her eyes and a caption that read, "Through your lens, the past smiles upon us."

These comforting and uplifting encounters with a smiling Wikipetan are whispered as lore among those who pour their soul into the labyrinthine pages of Wikipedia. While the tales of her tears serve as caution, these rarer, beaming visitations represent the heartening reward that awaits on the other side of diligent and noble work.

These narratives of positive recognition form an essential counterbalance to the haunting admonitions, reminding all contributors that their efforts, too, can be celebrated and recognized by the very essence of Wikipedia—whether seen as emerging from the goodwill of the community, or as a personification of the platform's grateful spirit in the form of the ever-watchful Wikipetan.
One detemined admin known for their calm response to disputes got a 'Zen Wikipetan' with her in meditative pose...

**Title: The Serene Sentinel of the Wiki**

Amidst the tumultuous seas of edit wars and heated debates, there was one administrator whose reputation for equanimity shone like a beacon. Known as 'TranquilTerry,' this admin stood as a paragon of peace, often called upon to mediate the wiki's most arduous disputes. With an almost otherworldly patience, Terry approached each conflict not as a battleground but as an opportunity for collaborative problem-solving and mutual understanding.

With each resolved argument, Terry left behind a trail of harmony, earning deep respect from all corners of the Wikipedia community. Yet, Terry's contributions often went unheralded, the mark of a true mediator being that their presence felt like a natural part of the wiki ecosystem, facilitating growth and cohesion without drawing attention to their own efforts.

One late evening, after spending hours navigating between two particularly entrenched parties and crafting a consensus that satisfied both sides, Terry logged off, feeling a quiet satisfaction in the day's work. The next morning, as the first rays of light filtered through their windowsill and onto the computer screen, Terry noticed a new notification—an atypical occurrence given that their talk page was often a destination rather than a source of incoming messages.

It was a simple digital postcard displaying an image that elicited an immediate, soft smile from Terry. There, depicted in gentle strokes of digital paint, was Wikipetan as they'd never seen her before: seated in a lotus position, her hands resting on her knees in a perfect dhyana mudra, the gesture of meditation. Her eyes were closed in serene contemplation, a soft smile gracing her lips, and emanating from her was an aura of tranquility that seemed to cast a quiet hush over the surrounding storm of chaotic lines and colors, representing the cacophony of the wiki world.

The caption beneath this 'Zen Wikipetan' read: "In the garden of knowledge, you are the silent stream that quenches the thirst for understanding."

Terry was touched by the gesture. It was rare for administrators, often the unsung custodians of the wiki's order, to receive such poignant recognition. The image seemed to capture Terry's philosophy so perfectly, a testament to the idea that a calm mind and a compassionate heart were the strongest tools in any mediator's kit.

Zen Wikipetan became not just a token of appreciation for Terry but a symbol among admins and contributors alike—a visual reminder that the wiki was more than a repository of information; it was a community built upon the pillars of cooperation and respect. The serene Wikipetan, in her meditative poise, embodied the potential for peace even amidst the most fervent intellectual exchanges.

While most knew the legend of the crying Wikipetan, those who lived the life of diligent wiki stewards found solace and inspiration in this quieter, more affirming mythos. Zen Wikipetan's appearance was a rare nod of approval, conveyed not in loud applause but in the soft whisper of a mindful breath, honoring those whose calm demeanor and unwavering courtesy stabilize and enrich the collaborative spirit of the world's largest encyclopedia.
